chapters.title: Many women feel a sense of failure if they’re unable to carry a child to term
chapters.text:
In Gabrielle Union’s first book, she bravely opened up about her struggle with fertility issues after several rounds of unsuccessful IVF (in vitro fertilization) following many miscarriages. People reached out to her and told her their stories of fertility problems, showing Union that she was far from alone. However, that didn’t stop her from feeling isolated and heartbroken throughout the process.

Union is older than her husband, Dwayne Wade, which seemed to be a huge sticking point with general society. Rather insensitively, many people would reach out and ask if the couple was finally going to have a baby. When they learned of her fertility issues, they asked if she would consider surrogacy, making her feel judged for prioritizing her career or leaving it to start a family too late. Whenever anyone mentioned surrogacy, it made her feel like a failure and that she should let another woman do the job because her body was incapable of doing so.

One of the most hurtful things she heard was that she had stolen the chance for her husband to have another child because she was almost ten years older than him. However, people didn’t know that she hadn’t “put it off” at all. Her doctor diagnosed her with adenomyosis after several rounds of IVF, telling her she likely had it since she was in her 20s, yet it had been missed all this time.

Countless women struggle to gain a diagnosis of endometriosis and other female reproductive issues. As a result, the condition deteriorates to the point where conception is almost impossible.

Many women have unanswered questions about the process and never really hear the truth. Through this summary of Union’s honest words, you’ll learn about her fertility battle and experience with surrogacy and finally understand the reality of such a personal journey.

chapters.title: The surrogacy journey is full of twists and turns
chapters.text:
At first, Gabrielle Union didn’t want to think about surrogacy, wanting to be pregnant and carry her baby to term. She felt like a failure and continued to put herself through the trauma and discomfort of IVF, even questioning whether she should leave her husband and allow him to find someone who could give him a child. The idea of handing over the responsibility to another woman, a woman somehow deemed more capable, and not experiencing childbirth herself was heartbreaking. At a consultation following another failed IVF attempt, her doctor told her that her best chance of a healthy baby was to try surrogacy. The couple discussed it and decided to try after Wade said he couldn’t watch her put herself through more discomfort and pain.

They interviewed several surrogates and settled on Natalie. She felt drawn to Natalie and her husband as they seemed like good people with positive auras. When the embryo transfer was done, Union remembers seeing a burst of light as they smiled at one another. Although they would have to wait to take a pregnancy test, she somehow knew that her life was about to change.

She was right. Natalie was pregnant, and a baby girl was to be born around Thanksgiving.

It is okay to keep your surrogacy private to help reduce stress on the surrogate and baby.

Despite her joy, Gabrielle Union wouldn’t allow herself to get attached to her unborn child. She was terrified that the baby could be snatched away and was too aware of the complications that could occur. It wasn’t until five months into the pregnancy that she allowed herself to start looking forward to the baby’s arrival. They also settled on a name — Kaavia James. Wade had a tattoo of his unborn daughter’s name on his shoulder, and, although terrified of its permanency, Union tried to push her worries away.

I realized I was ready to parent well, but not to mother. I’d made sure Kaavia James got the very best womb with a hip surrogacy family who played the Earth, Wind & Fire, Hall & Oates and Luther Vandross I requested. But I kept her at arm’s length because she could still be snatched away. ~ Gabrielle Union

chapters.title: A much-wanted child finally arrived in the world
chapters.text:
When Union told her father about Kaavia James’ impending arrival, he was confused and immediately looked down at her flat stomach. That action was like a knife in her heart; she still felt like a failure. Her father asked if she would let the surrogate see “her kid,” clearly wholly misunderstanding the concept of surrogacy. He then joked about her finding someone to “get the job done,” and his words hurt.

Union tried to push all of this away and keep her mind on positive thoughts. Then, before Thanksgiving, her surrogate called to say that her waters had broken. She was in labor for 38 hours before having an emergency cesarean; the umbilical cord had wrapped around the baby’s neck, putting both in danger.

Upon hearing Kaavia James’ first cry in the world, she knew that this baby had been loved even before conception. But, even though she adored her daughter, Union couldn’t help but feel a little disconnected. She tried skin-on-skin bonding straight after the birth, but she felt almost as if she was looking in on someone else’s life.

Many women who use surrogacy expect to feel connected to their child immediately. But for many, there is a sense of disconnection for a short while, which is entirely normal.

Union felt like she was doing everything wrong and worried she was to blame whenever the baby cried. But Wade didn’t seem to have the same problem. He was already a parent from his first marriage, and upon seeing Kaavia James for the first time, he connected immediately.

Looking back on Kaavia James’ birth and her first few months of life, Union now knows that she can be honest about her feelings without guilt or concern. She often wondered whether the little girl would love her more if she had been the one to carry her, but now she knows that’s not the case. Watching Kaavia James grow shows that she made the very best choice every day.

Did you know? Kim Kardashian is another celebrity who opted for surrogacy with her last two children, following complicated pregnancies with the first two.

chapters.title: Rape destroyed Gabrielle Union, but she rose from the ashes
chapters.text:
It’s easy to assume that celebrities have charmed childhoods or manage to side-step every problem that comes their way. But that’s often untrue. In 1992, Gabrielle Union was held at gunpoint and raped in a wealthy neighborhood in California, resulting in her long-time struggle with PTSD.

After the rape, Union couldn’t function. She hid in her room and struggled with every aspect of what she had been through. Her parents had moved the family to Pleasanton in California because it was a well-to-do area, and they thought that they would all be safe there. She always felt that she didn’t quite fit in as a woman of color compared to the predominantly white majority, yet they assumed living in an affluent place would protect her. Unfortunately, that hadn’t been the case, and her parents carried a lot of guilt as a result.

Gabrielle Union has been open about her rape and has lobbied Congress to overhaul the state legislature.

Despite her bravery, she still feels that the old version of her died that day, and she was reborn out of the ashes.

While I was being raped, I left my body and looked down at that girl. This doesn’t happen to good people, I thought. When that illusion was taken from me, I was left completely exposed, literally stripped and violated. ~ Gabrielle Union

She remembers staring at the TV and not taking anything in, yet the Olympics somehow managed to permeate her psyche. Watching people overcome the odds and achieve made her heart start beating once more. She felt there was a slight sign of life returning. As she sat watching the same Olympic events on TV several years later with her family, the ghost entered the room, milled around, and walked back out again.

At that time, she realized the ghost of the past, the ghost of who she used to be, would always be with her.

Do not let the ghosts of the past make you scared of the present. They tend to walk away when you come to terms with them.

chapters.title: The outdated view of older women with younger men still endures
chapters.text:
Gabrielle Union and several of her friends are older than their husbands. They have something else in common — society loves to judge.

Union doesn’t like to think about growing older. She often plays around and has fun, not caring too much about the numbers on her birth certificate and what they mean. One particular day, she was joking around with her husband, and she went to straddle him playfully. They both stopped in shock when they heard a large cracking noise from her ankle. Oh, the joys of aging!

Her husband laughed and said that he thought they would have more time.

Gabrielle Union is ten years older than her husband, Dwayne Wade, a professional NBA basketball player.

Union isn’t particularly concerned about their age gap, yet she does admit to sometimes having insecurities, mostly centered around what society has to say about it. Whenever she features in a magazine and journalists mention her husband, they always point out the age difference. She questions whether they would do the same if she were younger than him.

In this day and age, it’s almost as if women aren’t allowed to have the audacity to age with grace. Instead, they’re pushed toward surgery and other innovations to make themselves appear younger. It’s nothing short of stressful!

Union talked about a female friend whose younger husband left her. Considering the age difference, everyone felt it was inevitable and thought she should have expected it. Her friend assumed she had to accept it as “just one of those things.” Yet this is how society has made us think about women who are older than their men.

Union states that women should have the bravery and grace to age however they want to rather than this apparent inequality. They should have hope and believe in love, whether their husband is older, younger, or the same age.

chapters.title: Forget finding balance. Instead, find your own sense of ease
chapters.text:
Being good at career and family matters, staying attractive, and never getting tired are parts of being female that annoy every woman. Whether they have children or not, many women feel they’re always chasing their tails, constantly borrowing time from something or someone else to manage everything.

Everything you choose to do usually has to be at the expense of something or someone else. If you’re working, you’re not spending time with your family. If you’re with your family, you’re neglecting your career. It’s exhausting! It leaves you feeling spent, constantly overdrawn, and never breaking even.

Your life is not just a hamster wheel. It’s an elaborate but creaky system of about five wheels you are expected to keep moving with precision timing. ~ Gabrielle Union

It’s common to feel like everything will collapse around you if you make one wrong move, almost like a stack of dominoes waiting to fall. But despite all of this, you keep going because you have no other choice. Instead, people tell you that you’re supposed to find balance, but what is that exactly?

Gabrielle Union tells us that balance is nothing but a myth. She’s attempted to find balance herself several times over yet always failed. But men don’t seem to have this problem; they delegate tasks, so balance is no issue. If a woman focuses on her career, she pushes the chance of a family down the line or gives the responsibility of looking after her child to someone else. She can’t win!

Women are expected to fulfill specific criteria and to have their priorities in the “right place.” But who decided what these priorities should be? Decide for yourself.

If this is your situation, and that’s probably the case for many women, forget about balance. Throw it out of the window because it simply doesn’t exist. Instead, surround yourself with people who will help, pick up the slack, and support you. Of course, that also means you do the same for them. It’s about asking for grace, not finding an impossible balance.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Gabrielle Union did not have an easy childhood. She hasn’t had an easy adulthood either, but she is a shining beam of hope for anyone struggling with many issues that affect women today. By understanding her journey, you can take heart from the fact that it’s possible to overcome anything.

When Union had miscarriage after miscarriage and unsuccessful IVF after unsuccessful IVF, she felt like a failure, her body incapable of doing the one thing it was supposed to do. Yet the truth is that many women experience the same thing, and it is not their fault. Women are unreasonably expected to hit certain social “norms,” and people judge and whisper when they fail to do so.

Life’s struggles help to shape who we are and allow us to build and become stronger. When Union was raped, it devastated every single part of her being. Rather than allowing it to define her life, she slowly built herself back up with support. Then, when she was strong enough, she used her fame as a platform to lobby congress and help survivors of rape.

Through every struggle Gabrielle Union has faced, she has somehow managed to turn it into a positive in the end. Her story shows us that celebrities we place high on a pedestal are, in the end, just like us. They go through trials and tribulations, struggle with society’s expectations, and feel the same emotions as we do. So, be kind to everyone around you. You never know what battles they are facing.

Try this

• Dedicate one day per week to self-care. Self-care Sunday or whatever you want to call it. Do everything you enjoy, and do not feel guilty about it!
• Societal expectations will always be around, but understand that you do not have to be affected by them. Focus on yourself and what you want; the only person who can live your life is you.

Read it till the end?
